title: Open a Bank Account in the Netherlands
metaTitle: Open a Bank Account in the Netherlands | Expat Banking Guide
subtitle: A practical guide for expats opening a Dutch bank account, including requirements, BSN rules, bank comparisons, and the best options for internationals.
description: Learn how to open a bank account in the Netherlands as an expat. Requirements, BSN rules, costs, best banks, and practical tips for setting up your finances after arrival.

image.alt: An expat man in glasses works on his banking setup with a laptop and paperwork at a wooden desk, overlooking a scenic canal and traditional buildings in the Netherlands. The desk includes documents, passports, Euro currency, and guidebooks labeled Banking, Residence Permit, and Registration, illustrating the process of opening a bank account for foreigners.

heroCta.title: After Arriving in the Netherlands
heroCta.supportingText: Plan your first steps after arrival, including registration, BSN, and banking.

tocItems.label: Why expats need a Dutch bank account
Do you need a BSN first?

quickAnswers.label: BSN Required
quickAnswers.value: Most banks require a BSN, though some allow opening an account first and submitting the BSN later.

quickAnswers.label: Address Needed
quickAnswers.value: A Dutch residential address is usually required for verification.

quickAnswers.label: Opening Time
quickAnswers.value: Online accounts can be opened within minutes to a few days.

quickAnswers.label: Monthly Fees
quickAnswers.value: Typical bank accounts cost €3–€5 per month.

sections.heading: Introduction
body: Opening a Dutch bank account is usually one of the first practical steps after moving to the Netherlands.
You will likely need a local account to receive your salary, pay rent, set up health insurance, pay for subscriptions and utilities, and use iDEAL payments online.

Most banks require a passport or valid ID, a Dutch address, a BSN (citizen service number), and a residence permit if applicable. However, some banks allow accounts to be opened before receiving a BSN as long as the number is provided later.

sections.heading: Why Expats Need a Dutch Bank Account
body: Most financial activities in the Netherlands rely on local banking. You will typically need a Dutch account for salary payments, rent payments, health insurance premiums, taxes, utilities, and online payments using iDEAL.
Card payments are widely used and some businesses only accept debit cards linked to Dutch accounts.

sections.heading: Requirements to Open a Bank Account
body: Banks typically ask for a standard set of documents. You receive a BSN after registering with a municipality, so planning your registration appointment early helps with banking setup.
bullets: Proof of income or employment in some cases

links.label: Universiteit van Amsterdam – Opening a Dutch bank account

sections.heading: Do You Need a BSN First?
body: Most banks require a BSN for regulatory and tax reporting reasons. However, some banks allow opening the account first and submitting the BSN within about 90 days.
Examples include ABN AMRO, bunq, and ING—but this depends on your passport type and the bank’s current onboarding process. Always check the latest requirements on the bank’s website.

callout.title: BSN and timing
callout.text: If you do not yet have a BSN, look for providers that allow you to start the process and provide your BSN later. EUR.nl notes that some banks allow this; confirm directly with the bank.

sections.heading: Best Banks in the Netherlands for Expats
body: The table below compares expat-friendly options. Digital banks often allow faster onboarding and may be easier if you are still waiting for documents or prefer a mobile-first setup.

sections.heading: Comparison of Major Banks in the Netherlands
body: Below is a detailed comparison of major banks and financial platforms used by expats in the Netherlands. Includes overview, main features, pros and cons, typical costs, and links to each provider. Always confirm current offers and requirements on the bank’s website.

bankComparisons.name: ABN AMRO
bankComparisons.overview: One of the largest Dutch banks, with dedicated expat services, English-language information, and a strong branch network. Popular with international workers and employers for salary accounts.
features: Full current account with iDEAL and Maestro
May allow account opening before BSN in some cases
pros: Widely recognised; many employers use ABN AMRO
Clear expat information and dedicated pages
cons: Account approval can take a few days
Some products require BSN and proof of address
bankComparisons.typicalCosts: Around €4/month for a basic current account; premium and packaged accounts cost more.

bankComparisons.name: ING
bankComparisons.overview: The largest bank in the Netherlands by customers, with a broad branch and ATM network. Offers English-language options and is commonly used for everyday banking and salary.
pros: Very large network; easy to find ATMs and branches
cons: BSN and Dutch address usually required
Less expat-specific onboarding than ABN AMRO
bankComparisons.typicalCosts: Around €3–€4/month for a basic account; packaged and premium options available.

bankComparisons.name: Rabobank
bankComparisons.overview: A major Dutch cooperative bank with a strong presence nationwide. Offers full banking services; often chosen by people who prefer a traditional bank with a cooperative structure.
features: Current and savings accounts, mortgages, loans
Dutch-focused but with options for internationals
cons: Less expat-oriented than ABN AMRO or ING
BSN and address typically required from the start
bankComparisons.typicalCosts: Around €3–€5/month for a basic account; package deals may reduce fees.

bankComparisons.name: bunq
bankComparisons.overview: Dutch-licensed digital bank with a strong focus on expats and internationals. App-based onboarding, English throughout, and often faster account opening; some options allow starting before you have a BSN.
features: Free and paid plans; multiple sub-accounts
Fast onboarding; possible to open before BSN in some cases
pros: Very fast, simple sign-up for many users
Useful for early arrival when other banks are slower
cons: Paid features can add up if you use premium options
Not a full-service bank for mortgages or complex products
bankComparisons.typicalCosts: Free tier available; paid plans from around €2.99–€3.99/month and up.

bankComparisons.name: Wise
bankComparisons.overview: Financial platform (not a full bank in the Netherlands) offering multi-currency accounts and Dutch IBAN for receiving and sending money. Often used for international transfers and as a complement to a local bank.
features: Dutch IBAN for receiving euro payments
Useful for salary, rent, or moving money to the Netherlands
pros: Low, transparent fees for international transfers
Dutch IBAN without needing a traditional bank first
Good for receiving salary or rent from abroad
cons: Not a full bank; no iDEAL or full Dutch banking suite
Some employers or landlords may prefer a traditional bank IBAN
Best used alongside a Dutch bank for local payments
bankComparisons.typicalCosts: No monthly account fee; pay per transfer and conversion; card and other fees apply depending on use.

sections.heading: Traditional Banks vs Digital Banks
body: Traditional banks such as ABN AMRO, ING, and Rabobank offer branches, mortgages and loans, and full financial services. They suit people who want in-person support and a full range of products.
Digital banks such as bunq, Wise, and N26 offer fast onboarding, mobile-first apps, and often easier international transfers. They can be a good first step or complement to a traditional account.
bullets: Traditional: ABN AMRO, ING, Rabobank — branches, mortgages, full services.
Digital: bunq, Wise, N26 — fast onboarding, mobile-first, easier international transfers.

sections.heading: Business Bank Accounts
body: Entrepreneurs and freelancers may need a business account in addition to a personal one. Requirements usually include a KvK registration number, business documents, and tax information. Business accounts can take longer to approve than personal accounts, so plan ahead.

sections.heading: Typical Costs
body: Monthly fees vary by provider and plan. Extra costs may apply for international transfers, credit cards, or premium accounts.

sections.heading: Useful Services
body: These services are commonly used by expats for banking, insurance, housing, and mobile setup during relocation. Compare options and confirm terms on the providers’ sites.

services.name: bunq
services.description: Dutch digital bank with expat-friendly onboarding and English support; useful for opening an account quickly.

services.name: Wise
services.description: Multi-currency account and international transfers; useful for moving money into the Netherlands and managing foreign currency.

services.name: Independer
services.description: Insurance comparison site often used to compare Dutch health insurance and other insurance options.
services.reason: Useful when evaluating health insurance after arrival.

services.name: Simyo
services.description: Dutch SIM-only mobile provider; useful once you want a local number for banking and admin.

services.name: HousingAnywhere
services.description: Temporary and medium-term rentals often used by internationals before or after arrival.
services.reason: Relevant when you need an address for registration and banking.

sections.heading: Official Sources
body: Use these official sources to confirm current requirements and procedures. Bank rules and product details can change.

exampleScenarios.title: Highly Skilled Migrant
exampleScenarios.summary: Needs a salary account quickly. Prioritise a bank that offers fast onboarding and accepts your documents; traditional banks like ABN AMRO or ING are often used.

exampleScenarios.title: International Student
exampleScenarios.summary: Needs a free or low-cost student account. Check with your institution and compare bunq, ING, or other providers that offer student-friendly options.

exampleScenarios.title: Entrepreneur
exampleScenarios.summary: Needs both personal and business accounts. Plan for KvK registration and allow extra time for business account approval.

exampleScenarios.title: Family Relocating
exampleScenarios.summary: Needs joint accounts and budgeting. Consider banks that support multiple account holders and straightforward online banking.

scenariosSectionIntro: Different situations lead to different banking priorities. The cards below link to guides that help you plan your first steps.

faq.q: Can foreigners open a bank account in the Netherlands?
faq.a: Yes. Many banks in the Netherlands allow non-residents and expats to open an account. Requirements typically include a valid ID, Dutch address, and usually a BSN (citizen service number). Some banks let you start the process before you have a BSN and submit it later.

faq.q: Do I need a BSN to open a bank account?
faq.a: Most banks require a BSN for regulatory and tax reasons. Some banks, including ABN AMRO, bunq, and ING, may allow you to open an account first and provide your BSN within a set period (often around 90 days). Check the bank’s current policy.

faq.q: Which bank is easiest for expats?
faq.a: Digital banks such as bunq often offer the fastest, app-based onboarding and full English support. Traditional banks like ABN AMRO and ING also have expat-oriented products and English information. Ease depends on your documents and whether you already have a BSN and address.

faq.q: How long does it take to open a bank account?
faq.a: Online accounts can often be opened within minutes to a few days, depending on the provider and whether you have all documents ready. Traditional banks may take a few days to a week. Business accounts can take longer.

faq.q: Can students open a Dutch bank account?
faq.a: Yes. Students can open a Dutch bank account; many banks offer student-friendly or low-cost options. You will typically need proof of enrolment, ID, and usually a BSN and Dutch address. Check with your institution and the bank for current requirements.

Yes. Entrepreneurs and freelancers can open business accounts. You will usually need a KvK registration number, business documents, and tax information. Approval can take longer than for personal accounts, so plan ahead.
